items.title: Definition and classification
items.contentHtml:
Definition and classification of hyperopia

  • Hyperopia: refractive defect in which parallel light rays converge behind the retina in an eye without accommodation.
  • Classification according to magnitude: low (<+2.00 D), medium (+2.00 to +5.00 D), high (>+5.00 D).
  • Clinical classification: simple hyperopia (axial, curvature, index), pathological (due to corneal or lens alterations), functional (due to accommodative paralysis).
  • Classification by accommodative state: latent (compensable by ciliary tone), manifest (non-compensable, subdivided into optional and absolute).

items.foco: Etiology: short axis or weak refractive power
items.title: Etiology
items.contentHtml:
Etiology: short axis or weak refractive power

  • Most common cause: short anteroposterior axis (axial hyperopia).
  • Other causes: flat cornea (decreased refractive power), absent lens (aphakia) or posteriorly dislocated, low refractive index of the crystalline lens.
  • In microphthalmos or nanopthalmos there is high hyperopia (>+8.00 D) associated with the risk of narrow-angle glaucoma.
  • The normal axial length is ~23.5 mm; in axial hyperopia it is usually <22.5 mm.

items.title: Symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Symptoms: blurred near vision, asthenopia

  • In young people with low hyperopia: accommodative asthenopia (frontal headache, eye fatigue, tearing) when reading or working close up.
  • In moderate/high hyperopia: blurred near vision (difficulty focusing on close objects) and, if accommodation is insufficient, also distance vision blurred.
  • Symptoms worsen with age due to loss of accommodative amplitude (early presbyopia).
  • Children may be asymptomatic due to high accommodative capacity, but present convergent accommodative strabismus or amblyopia.

items.title: Latent vs manifest
items.contentHtml:
Latent vs manifest hyperopia

  • Latent hyperopia: compensated by the tone of the ciliary muscle; not detected without cycloplegia; it is usually <+1.00 D in young adults.
  • Many hyperopia: cannot be compensated by accommodation; It is subdivided into facultative (can be compensated voluntarily) and absolute (not compensable, produces blurred vision).
  • The sum of latent + manifest hyperopia = total hyperopia (measured under cycloplegia).
  • ENARM trap: in patients > 40 years old, latent hyperopia decreases and manifests it. increases.

items.title: Diagnosis: retinoscopy with cycloplegia
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic test: retinoscopy with cycloplegia

  • Gold standard: retinoscopy under cycloplegia (for example cyclopentolate 1% or atropine 1% in children).
  • Cycloplegia eliminates accommodation and reveals total hyperopia (latent + manifest).
  • In retinoscopy, the movement of the reflex is “in favor” (with) in hyperopia; It is neutralized with positive lenses.
  • Without cycloplegia, hyperopia is underestimated, especially in children and young adults.

items.title: Correction with convex lenses
items.contentHtml:
Correction with convex (positive) lenses

  • The convex (positive) lenses converge the rays before entering the eye, moving the focus towards the retina.
  • The lowest positive power that gives the best distance visual acuity is prescribed, so as not to force accommodation.
  • In young patients with asymptomatic low hyperopia, no correction required; in symptomatic patients or with strabismus, yes.
  • For near correction, positive addition can be added (bifocal or progressive prescription).

items.title: Calculation of the spherical lens
items.contentHtml:
Lens calculation: spherical lens formula

  • The power of the lens is calculated with the spherical lens formula: P = 1/f (in meters).
  • For a hyperopic eye, the necessary lens is the one that neutralizes the excess convergence required: P = + (total D).
  • Example: if the retinoscopy under cycloplegia gives +3.50 D, +3.00 D is prescribed (leaving +0.50 D of residual latent hyperopia).
  • In aphakia (absence of lens) lenses of approximately +10.00 to +12.00 D are required.

items.foco: Hyperopia in children: risk of accommodative strabismus
items.title: Hyperopia in children and strabismus
items.contentHtml:
Hyperopia in children: risk of accommodative strabismus

  • Excessive accommodation to compensate for hyperopia triggers accommodative convergence, producing accommodative convergent strabismus (esotropia).
  • High AC/A ratio (accommodation-convergence/accommodation) predisposes to this strabismus.
  • Treatment is total correction with positive lenses under cycloplegia; In many cases the esotropia resolves.
  • If it is not corrected, the child can develop strabic amblyopia.

items.title: Amblyopia due to hyperopia
items.contentHtml:
Amblyopia due to uncorrected hyperopia

  • High uncorrected hyperopia (>+3.00 D) in children can cause bilateral refractive amblyopia (due to bilateral defocus) or anisometropic if it is unilateral (difference >+1.50 D between eyes).
  • The brain suppresses the blurred image of the eye. more farsighted eye, leading to amblyopia.
  • Diagnosis: low visual acuity without organic cause; treatment: complete optical correction and occlusion of the healthy eye if necessary.
  • Critical treatment window: before 7-8 years of age; Afterwards it is irreversible.

items.foco: Treatment: glasses, contact lenses or refractive surgery
items.title: Treatment: options
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: glasses, contact lenses or refractive surgery

  • First line: glasses with convex lenses (simple, safe, modifiable).
  • Contact lenses: provide a better field of vision and less aberration; indicated in anisometropia or high hyperopia.
  • Refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, LASEK, phakic lens implantation) for stable patients (>21 years, stable refraction >1 year).
  • In high hyperopia (>+6.00 D) phakic lens implantation or lens extraction with intraocular lens implantation.

items.foco: LASIK and PRK surgery in hyperopia
items.title: LASIK and PRK in hyperopia
items.contentHtml:
LASIK and PRK surgery in hyperopia

  • LASIK: a corneal flap is created and the periphery is ablated to flatten the center (hypermetropic profile).
  • PRK: the epithelium is removed and peripheral ablation is performed; indicated in thin corneas or flap contraindication.
  • Typical treatable range: up to +4.00 to +5.00 D; less predictable results than in myopia.
  • Complications: regression, corneal ectasia (rare), halos and glare at night due to a small optical zone.

items.title: Complication: narrow angle glaucoma
items.contentHtml:
Complications: narrow angle glaucoma

  • Hypermetropic eyes have shallow anterior chamber and narrow iridocorneal angle, predisposing to narrow angle glaucoma (acute or chronic).
  • Triggering factors: pharmacological mydriasis (cycloplegic, antidepressants), darkness, stress.
  • Clinical: eye pain, blurred vision, colored halos, nausea; intraocular pressure >40 mmHg.
  • Acute treatment: acetazolamide 500 mg intravenously, mannitol 20% 1-2 g/kg intravenous, pilocarpine 2% topical (if no synechiae), YAG laser iridotomy.
